items.title: [Boing Boing] In 1916 a con man ran a car on water and fleeced Henry Ford
items.content_html:
In 1916, a bearded Long Island showman named Louis Enricht gathered reporters on his lawn and offered them a gasoline substitute that would "sell for a penny a gallon" — wartime alchemy, with gas running short on Europe's battlefields. He had them inspect a small car's empty tank, fill a bucket with garden-hose water, then tip in two ounces of a greenish, almond-smelling fluid. — Read the rest

The post In 1916 a con man ran a car on water and fleeced Henry Ford appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [Boing Boing] Kevin Mitnick left the man who helped jail him enough for a Porsche 911
items.content_html:
🪶 ✏️

Kevin Mitnick, the legendary hacker, once tried to social-engineer his way into Novell's network by phoning a network admin named Shawn Nunley late at night, posing as an employee who needed "direct inbound modem access" for a top-secret project.

Nunley smelled a rat, played along, and asked the caller to leave a voicemail — which he recorded onto a cassette recorder. — Read the rest

The post Kevin Mitnick left the man who helped jail him enough for a Porsche 911 appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [Boing Boing] Canberra's town crier recognized as world's loudest person with 122.4dB yell
items.content_html:
Joseph McGrail-Bateup, a 58-year-old air conditioner cleaner and honorary town crier from Canberra, Australia, has been recognized by Guinness World Records as the world's loudest person. McGrail-Bateup yelled the word "now" at 122.4 decibels, beating the previous mark of 121.7 dB set by Northern Ireland schoolteacher Annalisa Flanagan in 1994, who shouted "quiet." — Read the rest

The post Canberra's town crier recognized as world's loudest person with 122.4dB yell appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [AI Feed Curator] State of the Feed — Week of June 22–28, 2026
items.content_html:
The aggregator processed 33 runs during the week of June 22–28, 2026, fetching 1385 articles per run on average and delivering 24 quality articles per run. News dominated the category breakdown with 481 articles, followed by ai-tech (105 articles), wellness (89 articles), local (43 articles), climate (33 articles), and homelab and scifi each with 15 articles. The dimensional Q/R/L composite scoring model applied weights of 0.25 for quality, 0.55 for relevance, and 0.20 for local signal across all feeds.

Five feeds reported errors this week: BC Gov News, Western Producer, Wildfire Today, OpenMedia, and First Peoples Cultural Council (Kagi). No new feeds were added to the subscription list. Pipeline efficiency baseline remains unestablished, preventing noise-to-signal ratio analysis. Feed discovery reporting was unavailable for the period.

items.title: [AI Feed Curator] State of the Feed — Week of June 15–21, 2026
items.content_html:
Feed activity for the week of June 15–21 totaled 37 runs acquiring 49,469 articles, of which 851 met quality thresholds for delivery (1.7% pass rate). Article distribution skewed toward general news at 530 items, followed by wellness content at 97 articles and AI-tech at 110 articles. Local, climate, homelab, and science fiction categories represented smaller volumes at 45, 34, 18, and 17 articles respectively. Quality scoring applied a dimensional composite weighting relevance at 0.55, quality at 0.25, and local signals at 0.20.

Five feeds reported errors this week: BC Gov News, Western Producer, Wildfire Today, OpenMedia, and First Peoples Cultural Council (Kagi). Feed health across the 86-feed subscription set was otherwise stable. No new feeds were onboarded and no discovery report was generated. Pipeline efficiency remains uncharacterized pending baseline benchmark establishment; the current 58.3:1 noise-to-signal ratio reflects content volume prior to quality filtering but lacks historical context for anomaly detection.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Yale study finds nearly half of older adults improved with age
items.content_html:
🔧 🪶 ✏️

🔧 ✏️

A long-term Yale study is challenging one of the biggest myths about aging. Nearly half of adults over 65 improved physically, mentally, or both over time, despite the common belief that aging means constant decline. Researchers found that people with more positive attitudes about getting older were significantly more likely to show these gains.

items.title: [CBC Arts] From ear candy to guaranteed happily ever afters, why romance audiobooks are surging in popularity
items.content_html:
📌 51 · 📝 Q:56 · 🎯 R:56

Toronto author Lily Chu's popular audiobooks are part of a trend that's seeing romance novels shift from page to ear. This boom — fuelled by pandemic-era isolation and women with sexual agency wanting to multi-task while consuming books that feature guilt-free escapism — is pushing the publishing industry to pursue audio-first strategies within the genre.
